summary: Mary Wollstonecraft argues that women are fundamentally human beings endowed with reason, and their current frivolous, weak state is the result of a flawed, male-designed education system. She asserts that true virtue and societal progress require women to be educated as rational companions rather than mere objects of desire. Furthermore, she critiques arbitrary power, hereditary wealth, and hierarchical professions for corrupting human morality.

principles.statement: Women must be educated to become rational companions to men, or they will halt the progress of knowledge and virtue.
principles.rationale: Truth must be common to all to influence society. If women do not understand why they should be virtuous through the use of their own reason, they cannot truly practice virtue or raise patriotic, moral children.
principles.source_quote: If woman isn’t fitted by education to become man’s companion, she will stop the progress of knowledge, because truth must be common to all; if it isn’t it won’t be able to influence how people in general behave.

principles.statement: Human pre-eminence consists in reason, and individual superiority consists in virtue.
principles.rationale: The perfection of human nature and our capacity for happiness are measured by the degree of reason, virtue, and knowledge we attain. Passions were given to us specifically so that by struggling with them, we might develop this reason.
principles.source_quote: What does man’s pre-eminence over the lower animals consist in? The answer is as clear as ‘A half is less than the whole’; it consists in reason. What acquirement raises one being above another? We spontaneously reply: virtue.

principles.statement: Arbitrary power and strict hierarchical rank are inherently destructive to morality.
principles.rationale: Power intoxicates weak humans. Professions built on strict rank and blind obedience (like kings, standing armies, and clergy) stifle individual reason and replace true virtue with either despotism or servility.
principles.source_quote: Man is weak, and all power intoxicates him; and the way power is misused proves that the more equality there is among men... the more virtue and happiness will reign in society.

frameworks.name: First Principles Questioning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When societal prejudices, customs, or arguments of 'expediency' have clouded reason and established unnatural distinctions.
steps: Strip away prevailing prejudices and artificial manners.
Ask plain, axiomatic questions about fundamental human nature (e.g., what separates humans from animals).
Set these simple axioms alongside the deviations that current circumstances and customs have brought.
Build your arguments on these foundational truths rather than accepting what is merely 'expedient'.
frameworks.source_quote: In the present state of society it seems that we have to go back to first principles in search of the simplest truths, and to fight against some prevailing prejudice for every inch of ground.

mental_models.name: The Barren Blooming (Flowers in Over-Rich Soil)
mental_models.description: A metaphor for how society currently educates women. By focusing entirely on superficial beauty and allure rather than intellectual strength, society produces a creature that is temporarily pleasing but ultimately weak and useless.
mental_models.example: Wollstonecraft compares women to flowers planted in soil that is too rich: 'strength and usefulness are sacrificed to beauty; and the flamboyant leaves, after giving pleasure to viewers, fade on the stalk, disregarded, long before it was the time for them to reach maturity.'

mental_models.name: The Chain of Despots
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding hierarchical organizations (like the military or clergy). In these systems, individuals do not use their own reason; they merely obey those above them and command those below them, creating a structure devoid of individual moral character.
mental_models.example: She describes every military body as 'a chain of despots who obey and give commands without using their reason, and become dead weights of vice and folly on the community.'

heuristics: Judge women as human creatures first, rather than focusing on their sex.
Despise the weak elegance of mind and aim instead for strength of mind and body.
Guarantee your independence by contracting your wants.

signature_quotes.text: For many years I have regarded independence as the great blessing of life, the basis of every virtue; and even if I end up living on a barren heath, I will always guarantee my independence by contracting my wants.
signature_quotes.context: From the dedicatory letter to M. Talleyrand-Périgord, explaining her personal motivations and values.

signature_quotes.text: If woman isn’t fitted by education to become man’s companion, she will stop the progress of knowledge, because truth must be common to all...
signature_quotes.context: Stating her main argument for the rights and education of women.

signature_quotes.text: I earnestly wish to point out what true dignity and human happiness consist in; I want to persuade women to aim at strength of mind and body...
signature_quotes.context: Outlining her goal to move women away from superficial 'feminine' graces.

signature_quotes.text: The most wisely formed society is the one whose constitution is based on the nature of man...
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against using 'prescription' (tradition) to justify depriving people of their natural rights.

signature_quotes.text: Man is weak, and all power intoxicates him; and the way power is misused proves that the more equality there is among men... the more virtue and happiness will reign in society.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing arbitrary power and the monarchy.

anti_patterns.description: Educating women merely to be 'alluring mistresses' and focusing on superficial accomplishments rather than cultivating their understanding.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It makes women weak, artificial, and cunning. When their brief bloom of beauty fades, they are left without inner resources, becoming useless to society and mere 'propagators of fools.'

anti_patterns.description: Relying on 'prescription' (tradition or custom) to justify the oppression of women or men.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It uses the sheer length of time an injustice has existed as an excuse to avoid testing it against abstract principles of reason and human rights.

anti_patterns.description: Rousseau's romanticization of the 'state of nature' and his belief that civilization and human reason introduced evil.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It contradicts the wisdom of God, who gave humans reason and passions specifically so they could improve themselves, learn good by struggling with evil, and rise above the brutes.
